params.text:
Recording, reflecting and post-incident support

What happens after an incident matters as much as the incident itself. Careful recording and honest reflection help the team understand what happened and prevent it happening again, while post-incident support looks after the wellbeing of everyone involved.

  • Record: write down clearly and factually what happened, and any restriction used.
  • Reflect: help the team learn what triggered the incident and what would help next time.
  • Support: check on the person, and on staff, after a distressing event.

Every use of a restrictive practice must be recorded and reviewed, so that its use can be understood and reduced over time.

Bringing it together

Well done for completing this awareness course on Positive Behaviour Support. You have explored what PBS is and the values of quality of life and respect at its heart; how behaviour has a function and communicates an unmet need; the proactive and person-centred strategies that help people live well; and least restrictive practice, restraint reduction and keeping everyone safe. After any incident, remember to record, reflect, and make sure the person and your colleagues receive the support they need.

Remember the key point from the start: this course covers knowledge and awareness only. PBS plans and functional assessments are developed and led by trained professionals, and your role is to follow each person's plan and work in the least restrictive way. Any physical intervention needs specific, accredited training and is always a last resort — never something to attempt from online learning alone. Speak to your manager about the PBS plans of the people you support, and carry these values into your everyday practice.
